elements.sent-2411-001: Identifying valuable measurements is one of the main challenges in computational inverse problems, often framed as the optimal experimental design (OED) problem.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we investigate nonlinear OED within a continuously-indexed design space.
elements.sent-2411-003: This is in contrast to the traditional approaches on selecting experiments from a finite measurement set.
elements.sent-2411-004: This formulation better reflects practical scenarios where measurements are taken continuously across spatial or temporal domains.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, optimizing over a continuously-indexed space introduces computational challenges.
elements.sent-2411-006: To address these, we employ gradient-flow and optimal transport techniques, complemented by adaptive strategy for interactive optimization.
elements.sent-2411-007: Numerical results on the Lorenz 63 system and Schr\"odinger equation demonstrate that our solver identifies valuable measurements and achieves improved reconstruction of unknown parameters in inverse problems.
elements.sent-2411-008: Often in physical sciences, measurements are taken to infer unknown parameters.
elements.sent-2411-009: Typically an optimization formulation is deployed to conduct the parameter reconstruction~\cite{T05,HPUU08 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In a generic setting, the optimization is given as: \begin{equation}\label{eqn: continuous loss} \vecsigma^*:= \arg\min_{\vecsigma\in \R^d}\, \text{Loss}[\vecsigma; \rho]\, \equiv \int_{\Omega} \left(\M(\theta; \vecsigma) - \text{data}(\theta) \right)^2\rho(\theta)\, \dd \theta\,,\end{equation} where $\vecsigma\in\mathbb{R ^d$ is the unknown parameter to be recovered.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The notation $\theta$ is the design variable from the design space $\Omega$ that includes all possible experimental designs.
elements.sent-2411-012: Here by design variable, we mean a set of parameters used to describe the configuration of the experiment, for instance source and detector locations and observation time.
elements.sent-2411-013: Often this design variable $\theta$ is continuously indexed, as usually seen in regression models \cite{KW59,KH21 and medical imaging \cite{K96,LR09 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Empirically more measurements are selected for variable of $\theta$ when $\rho(\theta)$ is large.
elements.sent-2411-015: The system's Parameter-to-Output (PtO) map is $\M,$ meaning $\M(\theta; \vecsigma)$ gives the output at measurement index $\theta \in \Omega$ when the system is parameterized by $\vecsigma \in \R^d$.
elements.sent-2411-016: Here we specify that the mapping $\M(\theta; \vecsigma)$ itself contains no noise.
elements.sent-2411-017: Correspondingly, $\text{data (\theta)$ is the experiment measurement at $\theta \in \Omega$ generated via ground-truth parameters $\vecsigma_{\text{true }$ with additive random noise $\epsilon$, i.e. $\text{data (\theta) = \M(\theta;\vecsigma_{\text{true ) +\epsilon$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: When the design measure $\rho$ is fixed, the optimization formulation \eqref{eqn: continuous loss returns an optimizer $\vecsigma^*$ that finds the smallest mismatch between the generated output and the real data, and thus $\vecsigma^*$ is regarded as a good approximation to the ground-truth parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Generally speaking, the reconstruction quality can depend on the choice of $\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Considering the number of experimental data and field measurements are expensive or time-consuming to collect, it is specially crucial to design a $\rho$ that reveals the importance of measurements.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Two-layer gradient flow algorithm} \label{brute-force alg} input: number of main and sub-routine iterations $T, T',$ time steps in main and sub-routine $\Delta t, \Delta t';$ initial particles $\theta_1^0, \dots, \theta_N^0 \subset \Omega$ and parameter $\vecsigma^{*,0} \in \R^d$ s.t. $\vecsigma^{*,0} = \arg\min_{\vecsigma } \text{Loss}[\vecsigma; \rho^0]$. output: empirical measure $\rho$ \eqref{eqn:particle_presentation} and inference parameter $\vecsigma^*.$ \begin{algorithmic}[1] \For{$t = 0:T-1$} \color{blue}\State $\theta_i^{t+1} \leftarrow \theta_i^{t} + \Delta t\, \dot{\theta_i} \Big\vert_{\rho^t, ~\vecsigma^{*,t}} $ as done in \eqref{eqn: particle update}-\eqref{eqn: particle velocity} for all $i = 1, \dots, N$ \State $\rho^{t+1} \leftarrow \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \delta_{\theta_i^{t+1}}$ \Comment{(I) update measure $\rho$} \color{red}\State initialize: $\vecsigma^{0} \leftarrow \vecsigma^{*, t} $\label{init line} \For{$t' = 0:T'-1$} \State $\vecsigma^{t'+1} \leftarrow \vecsigma^{t'} - \Delta t' \,\nabla_{\vecsigma}\, \text{Loss} [\vecsigma^{t'}; \rho^{t+1}]$ \EndFor \State\Return $\vecsigma^{*,t+1} \leftarrow \vecsigma^{T'}$ \Comment{(II) update parameter $\vecsigma^*$} \label{end line} \color{black} \EndFor \end{algorithmic} \Return $\rho \leftarrow \frac{1}{N} \sum_i^N \delta_{\theta_{i=1}^T} $ and $\vecsigma^{*} \leftarrow \vecsigma^{*,T}.$
elements.sent-2411-155: We remark in \cref{brute-force alg , at iteration $t,$ the outer-loop takes one gradient-descent step to move the particles to $\{\theta_i^{t+1 \},$ equivalently $\rho^{t+1 .$ Next for the inner-loop optimization on solving $\vecsigma^*[\rho^{t+1 ] \equiv \vecsigma^{*,t+1}$: line~\ref{init line - \ref{end line , we use the vanilla gradient-descent.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-160: However, it is natural to suspect that if the outer-layer update from $\rho^t$ to $\rho^{t+1}$ is small, the associated optimum $\vecsigma^{\ast,t}$ should not change much, so starting the optimization fresh in this inner-layer optimization for every outer-iteration is wasteful.
elements.sent-2411-161: Building on this intuition, in this subsection we provide a streamlined approach to re-use the computation from the previous iteration, and reduce the cost.

elements.sent-2411-163: The key idea is to track the evolution of $\vecsigma^*$ using the evolution of $\rho$ or its particle representation $\{\theta_i \}_{i=1 ^N$.}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Streamlined gradient flow algorithm } \label{relaxed alg} input: number of particles $N;$ number of iterations $T;$ time step $\Delta t;$ initial particles $\theta_1^0, \dots, \theta_N^0 \subset \Omega$ and parameter $\vecsigma^{*,0}$ s.t. $\vecsigma^{*,0} = \arg\min \text{Loss} [\vecsigma,\rho^0] $. output: empirical measure $\rho$ \eqref{eqn:particle_presentation} and parameter $\sigma^*.$ \begin{algorithmic}[1] \For{$t = 0:T-1$} \color{blue} \State $\theta_i^{t+1} \leftarrow \theta_i^{t} + \Delta t\, \dot{\theta_i} \Big\vert_{\rho^t, ~\vecsigma^{*,t}} $ for all $i = 1, \dots, N$ \State $\rho^{t+1} \leftarrow \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \delta_{\theta_i^{t+1}}$ \Comment{(I) update measure $\rho$} \color{red} \State $\vecsigma^{*,t+1} \leftarrow \vecsigma^{*,t} + \Delta t \, \dot{\vecsigma^*} \Big\vert_{ \rho^{t+1}, ~\vecsigma^{*,t}} $ \Comment{(II) update parameter $\vecsigma^*$} \label{line} \color{black} \EndFor \end{algorithmic} \Return{$\rho \leftarrow \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \delta_{\theta_i^{T}}, ~\vecsigma^* \leftarrow \vecsigma^{*,T}$} \label{alternative alg}

elements.sent-2411-190: We demonstrate the performance of our numerical solvers in this section using Lorenz 63 model~\cite{L63 as an example.}
elements.sent-2411-191: It is a simplified mathematical model for atmospheric convection and is nonlinear and aperiodic.
elements.sent-2411-192: Though deterministic, the solution nevertheless demonstrates chaotic phenomenon and thus is a widely studied model.
elements.sent-2411-193: To examine the model: suppose we can take measurements along trajectory of the solution to infer parameters in the model, when should we probe into the model so that the collected data ensure the most stable recovery? We provide our numerical study in this section, by both using \cref{brute-force alg and \cref{alternative alg .}}
elements.sent-2411-194: The model is three dimensional and writes as: \begin{equation}\label{eqn: L63} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \dot{x} = \alpha(y-x) \dot{y} = x(\gamma-z)-y \dot{z} = xy-\beta z, \end{array} \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-195: As at atmospheric mode, $x,y,z$ variables stand for respectively the convection rate, horizontal and vertical temperature variations, and $\alpha,\gamma,\beta$ are proportional to Prandtl number, Rayleigh number and dimension of the physical layer respectively.
elements.sent-2411-196: Throughout this section for computation, we set the ground-truth parameters are $\vecsigma_{\true =(\alpha_\true,\gamma_\true,\beta_\true) = (10,28,8/3)$.}

elements.sent-2411-200: Namely, the PtO map $\M$ is defined as \begin{equation}\M(\theta; \vecsigma)\,,\quadwith\quad \theta=(c,t)\in \Omega = \{x,y,z\} \times [0,3]\,,\end{equation} where $c$ represents which one of the three coordinates to be observed.

elements.sent-2411-203: The derivative terms with respect to the model parameters $\vecsigma = [\alpha, \gamma, \beta]$ can be explicitly computed.
elements.sent-2411-204: According to the definition of the Jacobian: \begin{equation}\label{eqn: L63 Mgrad} J \doteq \nabla_{\vecsigma} \M = \left[\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{\partial x}{\partial \alpha} \frac{\partial x}{\partial \gamma} \frac{\partial x}{\partial \beta} \frac{\partial y}{\partial \alpha} \frac{\partial y}{\partial \gamma} \frac{\partial y}{\partial \beta} \frac{\partial z}{\partial \alpha} \frac{\partial z}{\partial \gamma} \frac{\partial z}{\partial \beta} \end{array} \right]\,,\end{equation} and to compute them, we time-differentiate~\eqref{eqn: L63 to obtain: \begin{equation}\label{eqn: L63 derivatives} \frac{\rd}{\rd t}J = \left[\begin{array}{ccc} -\alpha \alpha 0 \gamma-z -1 -x y x -\beta \end{array} \right]\cdot J + \left[ \begin{array}{ccc} y-x 0 0 0 x 0 0 0 -z \end{array} \right]\,.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-367: In computational inverse problems, the optimal experimental design (OED) problem seeks the best locations in time and space at which to take measurements.
elements.sent-2411-368: We investigate the nonlinear OED problem in the context of continuously-indexed design space for the measurements.
elements.sent-2411-369: In contrast to traditional approaches that select experiments from a finite measurement set, a continuous design space is often a better reflection of practical experimental options, where there is considerable flexibility concerning where and when to take measurements.
elements.sent-2411-370: The continuously-indexed space introduces computational challenges, and we address them by employing gradient-flow and optimal transport techniques, complemented by an adaptive strategy for bi-level optimization.
elements.sent-2411-371: Numerical results on the Lorenz 63 system and Schr\"odinger equation demonstrate that our solver identifies good measurement times / locations and achieves improved reconstruction of unknown parameters in inverse problems.
elements.sent-2411-372: In the mathematical models of physical systems, unknown parameters in the differential equations can be inferred from carefully chosen measurements of the system.
elements.sent-2411-373: An optimization formulation is often used to reconstruct the unknown parameter~\cite{T05,HPUU08 .}
elements.sent-2411-374: This formulation can be stated generically as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eqn: continuous loss} \vecsigma^*:= \arg\min_{\vecsigma\in \R^d}\, \text{Loss}[\vecsigma; \rho]\, \equiv \int_{\Omega} \left(\M(\theta; \vecsigma) - \text{data}(\theta) \right)^2\rho(\theta)\, \dd \theta\,,\end{equation} where $\vecsigma\in\mathbb{R ^d$ is the parameter to be recovered and the other elements of the formulation are described in the rest of this paragraph.}
elements.sent-2411-375: The notation $\theta$ indicates the design variable, selected from a space $\Omega$ of possible experimental design choices.
elements.sent-2411-376: Specifically, $\theta$ describes the configuration of the experiment, for instance, source and detector locations and observation times.
elements.sent-2411-377: Often, $\theta$ is naturally ``continuously indexed," as usually seen in regression models \cite{KW59,KH21 and medical imaging \cite{K96,LR09 .}}
elements.sent-2411-378: For instance, the time at which a measurement is taken can be {\em any time within a certain interval, not just one of a finite number of choices.}
elements.sent-2411-379: Empirically, more measurements are selected for a value $\theta$ when its density $\rho(\theta)$ is larger.
elements.sent-2411-380: The formulation \eqref{eqn: continuous loss incorporates a Parameter-to-Output (PtO) map $\M,$ where $\M(\theta; \vecsigma)$ gives the output at measurement index $\theta \in \Omega$ when the system parameter is $\vecsigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-381: Corresponding to this map is the experimental measurement $\text{data (\theta)$ which in contrast to $\M(\theta; \vecsigma)$, may contain measurement noise.}
elements.sent-2411-382: Specifically, we assume that $\text{data (\theta)$ is generated via ground-truth parameters $\vecsigma_{\text{true }$ with additive random noise $\epsilon$, that is, $\text{data (\theta) = \M(\theta;\vecsigma_{\text{true ) +\epsilon$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-383: When the design measure $\rho$ is fixed, the solution of \eqref{eqn: continuous loss is a parameter $\vecsigma^*$ that finds the smallest weighted $L^2$ mismatch between the output predicted by the model and the actual observations.}
elements.sent-2411-384: This value $\vecsigma^*$ is taken to be a good approximation to the ground-truth parameter $\vecsigma_{\text{true }$.}
elements.sent-2411-385: Reconstruction quality depends in general on the choice of $\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-386: Considering that experimental data and field measurements are often expensive or time-consuming to collect, it is crucial to choose $\rho$ in the most effective possible way.
elements.sent-2411-387: In this paper, we develop an efficient solver to find the optimal design $\rho$ that yields high-quality reconstruction of $\vecsigma^*$ at low cost.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Nonlinear OED Bilevel Optimization: Gradient Flow with Accurate Inner Loop} \label{brute-force alg} input: number of particles $N$; number of outer-level and inner-level iterations $T$ and $T'$, resp.; time steps for outer-level and inner-level iterations $\Delta t$ and $\Delta t'$, resp.; initial particles $\theta_1^0, \dots, \theta_N^0 \subset \Omega$; initial parameter $\vecsigma^{*,0} \in \R^d$ such that $\vecsigma^{*,0} \in \arg\min_{\vecsigma } \text{Loss}[\vecsigma; \rho^0]$. output: estimates of optimal empirical measure $\rho$ (represented in the form \eqref{eqn:particle_presentation}) and corresponding inference parameter $\vecsigma^*[\rho]$. \begin{algorithmic}[1] \For{$t = 0:T-1$} \State $\theta_i^{t+1} \leftarrow \theta_i^{t} + \Delta t\, \dot{\theta_i} \Big\vert_{\rho^t, ~\vecsigma^{*,t}} $, see \eqref{eqn: particle update}-\eqref{eqn: particle velocity}, with $\vecsigma^{*,t}$ replacing $\vecsigma^*[\rho^t]$, \; $i = 1, \dotsc, N$ \State $\rho^{t+1} \leftarrow \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \delta_{\theta_i^{t+1}}$ \Comment{(I) update measure $\rho$} \State initialize: $\vecsigma^{0} \leftarrow \vecsigma^{*, t} $\label{init line} \For{$t' = 0:T'-1$} \State $\vecsigma^{t'+1} \leftarrow \vecsigma^{t'} - \Delta t' \,\nabla_{\vecsigma}\, \text{Loss} [\vecsigma^{t'}; \rho^{t+1}]$ \EndFor \State\Return $\vecsigma^{*,t+1} \leftarrow \vecsigma^{T'}$ \Comment{(II) update parameter $\vecsigma^*$} \label{end line} \EndFor \end{algorithmic} \Return $\rho \leftarrow \frac{1}{N} \sum_i^N \delta_{\theta_{i=1}^T} $ and $\vecsigma^{*} \leftarrow \vecsigma^{*,T}.$

elements.sent-2411-506: We demonstrate the performance of our numerical algorithms for optimal design based on the Lorenz 63 model~\cite{L63 .}
elements.sent-2411-507: This physical system is a simplified mathematical model for atmospheric convection that is nonlinear and aperiodic.
elements.sent-2411-508: Though deterministic in our set up, the model solution exhibits chaotic behavior, which is why it is widely studied in many scientific domains.
elements.sent-2411-509: Suppose we can take measurements along the trajectory of the solution to infer the parameters in the model.
elements.sent-2411-510: At which time points should we probe the model so that the collected data yields the most stable recovery? We apply the proposed algorithms of \cref{sec: method to thoroughly address this experimental design question.}
elements.sent-2411-511: The Lorenz 63 model is three-dimensional and is defined by the following nonlinear differential equations: \begin{equation}\label{eqn: L63} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \displaystyle \frac{\dd x}{\dd \tau} = \alpha(y-x) \displaystyle \frac{\dd y}{\dd \tau} = x(\gamma-z)-y \displaystyle \frac{\dd z}{\dd \tau} = xy-\beta z, \end{array} \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-052: \begin{equation}\label{eqn: L63} \left\{ {l} \displaystyle \frac{\dd x}{\dd \tau} = \alpha(y-x) \displaystyle \frac{\dd y}{\dd \tau} = x(\gamma-z)-y \displaystyle \frac{\dd z}{\dd \tau} = xy-\beta z,  \right.}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-512: The variables $x,y,z$ stand respectively for the convection rate and horizontal and vertical temperature variations.
elements.sent-2411-513: We use $\tau$ to denote the time variable in the Lorenz model.
elements.sent-2411-514: The model parameters $\alpha,\gamma,\beta$ are proportional to Prandtl number, Rayleigh number, and dimension of the physical layer, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-516: The initial values of the variables are fixed as $x(0) = 1.5$, $y(0) = -1.5$, and $z(0) = 25$.
elements.sent-2411-517: Numerical simulation of the model \eqref{eqn: L63 is conducted using forward Euler with step-size $\Delta \tau = 10^{-4}$.}
elements.sent-2411-518: Next, we describe experimental design for Lorenz model.
elements.sent-2411-519: To reconstruct the model parameters $\vecsigma = (\alpha, \gamma, \beta)$, the PtO map $\M$ \eqref{eqn: continuous loss is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\M(\theta; \vecsigma)\,,\quadwith  \theta=(c,\tau)\in \Omega = \{x,y,z\} \times [0,3], \,\, \vecsigma = (\alpha, \gamma, \beta) \in \R^3 \, .\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-614: Unlike the Lorenz system, which evolves in time, the simplified Schr\"{o dinger equation describes a steady-state solution, with measurements taken {\em in space .}}
elements.sent-2411-615: In experimental design, we are tasked to find the measurement locations that contain the best information to recover the unknown potential.
elements.sent-2411-616: The steady state Schr\"{o dinger model with zero boundary condition is \begin{equation}\label{eqn: schrodinger} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} u'' + \sigma\, u = S(x)\,, \quad x \in [0,1] u \Big\vert_{x = 0, 1} = 0\,, \end{array} \right.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-054: \begin{equation}\label{eqn: schrodinger} \left\{ {l} u'' + \sigma\, u = S(x)\,, \quad x \in [0,1] u \Big\vert_{x = 0, 1} = 0\,,  \right.}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-617: Different sources $S(x)$ are injected into the model and the corresponding model solutions $u(x)$ are taken to infer the unknown potential function $\sigma \in L^2([0,1])$.
elements.sent-2411-618: In our numerical experiment, we set the ground-truth potential $\sigma_\true$ to be a Gaussian mixture; details are given below.
elements.sent-2411-619: For the purpose of experimental design, we can exploit the form of the source $S(x)$ and the detector location.
elements.sent-2411-620: Specifically we can inject source at a location $\theta^1 \in [0,1]$ by setting $S(x) = \delta_{\theta^1}$, and measure the solution of the resulting model \eqref{eqn: schrodinger , denoted by $u_{\theta^1}$, at location $\theta^2 \in [0,1]$.}
elements.sent-2411-621: The design variable is therefore $\theta = (\theta^1, \theta^2) \in\Omega = [0,1]^2$ and the simulated measurement is in the form: \begin{equation}\M(\theta;\sigma ) = u_{\theta^1}(\theta^2) \in \R\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-622: Using a classical calculus-of-variable argument, one can compute the Fr\'echet derivative of $\M$ with respect to $\sigma$ to be \begin{equation}\frac{\delta \M(\theta;\sigma )}{\delta\sigma } = u_{\theta^1} \cdot v_{\theta^2}\,,\end{equation} where $u_{\theta^1}$ and $v_{\theta^2}$ solve the forward and adjoint models, which are obtained from the following equations: \begin{equation}forward: ~~\left\{ {ll} u_{\theta^1}'' + \sigma\, u_{\theta^1} = \delta_{\theta^1}, x \in [0,1] u_{\theta^1}= 0, x = 0,1,  \right. \quad adjoint: ~~\left\{ {ll} v_{\theta^2}'' + \sigma\, v_{\theta^2} = \delta_{\theta^2}, x \in [0,1] v_{\theta^2} = 0, x = 0,1.  \right.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-623: We use the finite-difference method to solve these equations numerically.
